identity: L-Glutamine (free amino acid, non-essential/conditionally-essential). Synonyms: Gln, Q, L-2-aminoglutaramic acid, levoglutamide. CAS 56-85-9. MW 146.15 g/mol. Formula C5H10N2O3. Contains a side-chain amide group (the labile moiety). Typically supplied as free crystalline L-glutamine (no common stable salt form for oral use). Stabilized derivatives exist for parenteral/cell-culture use (L-alanyl-L-glutamine / N-acetyl-glutamine) precisely because free Gln is hydrolytically unstable in water. In this formula it appears in CAPSULES (dry): 70/100/80 mg across variants A/B/C — i.e. a DRY phase, not currently in any liquid SKU.
r1_solubility: Aqueous solubility ~36 mg/mL (≈250 mM) at neutral pH, ~25C [Sigma/ChemicalBook, Tier 2; consistent with Arii 1999 working concentrations]. NOTE: glutamine is currently a DRY capsule ingredient, so R1 is moot as formulated. If it were ever moved into a liquid: at a notional 10-50 mg/mL intended use concentration, headroom = 36/intended. At ~10 mg/mL -> headroom ~3.6 (OK); at ~30 mg/mL -> headroom ~1.2 (TIGHT); above ~36 mg/mL -> BLOCK (saturation). Solubility is NOT the limiting property for glutamine — chemical stability (R2) is. Verdict on R1 as-formulated: N/A (dry); as-hypothetical-liquid at low conc: OK, at high conc: TIGHT/BLOCK.
r2_stability: Glutamine is INTRINSICALLY UNSTABLE in water: it undergoes intramolecular ring-closing deamidation (hydrolysis of the side-chain amide) to 5-pyrrolidone-2-carboxylic acid (pyroglutamate) WITH RELEASE OF AMMONIA. Kinetics are pseudo-first-order; activation energy ~98.7 kJ/mol at pH 6.41 [Arii K et al, Eur J Pharm Sci 1999;9(1):75-78, PMID 10493999, DOI 10.1016/s0928-0987(99)00047-0, Tier 1]. Maximum stability pH window = 5.0-7.5 [PMID 10493999, Tier 1] — the formula target pH ~5.8-6.2 sits squarely INSIDE the most-stable window, so acid/base catalysis is minimized but water-mediated hydrolysis still proceeds. Reported degradation extent at room temperature, pH ~6: pyroglutamate ~0.2% at 3 days, ~3.3% at 45 days [model-system data, Tier 2/3 secondary]; this implies a wet shelf-life of only weeks-to-low-months at ambient before meaningful loss + ammonia accumulation. This is exactly why glutamine is OMITTED from aqueous TPN and why stabilized dipeptides (Ala-Gln) are used instead [parenteral-nutrition literature, Tier 2]. NOT light- or O2-sensitive per se (no redox-labile group); the dominant pathway is thermal/hydrolytic. As a DRY powder it is stable for ~12-18+ months (hygroscopic-moderate; keep desiccated). STORAGE: keep dry, sealed, cool. Verdict: stable DRY; degradation-prone WET even within the ideal pH window — keep glutamine in the dry capsule phase, never the drink.
r3_conflicts: (1) Glutamine + reducing sugars (S2 Korean corn syrup/maltodextrin/D-ribose; F5/S5 ribose) -> MAILLARD: free glutamine is a MAJOR precursor of brown products/fluorophores because its amide hydrolyzes in-situ to ammonia, which is MORE reactive than the amino acid itself with reducing sugars [Pischetsrieder/Hellwig-type work: "Free glutamine as a major precursor of brown products and fluorophores in Maillard reaction systems," PMID 17006601, Tier 1; ribose is among the most browning-reactive sugars]. Mechanism: amide hydrolysis -> NH3 -> Amadori/melanoidin browning + glutamine loss. Mitigation: NEVER co-formulate glutamine with reducing sugars in water/heat — keep glutamine dry (current state) and keep it out of the dextrose ORS stick. (2) Glutamine + B6/P5P (capsule co-present) in solution + heat -> amino-carbonyl/Schiff-base side reactions are possible but glutamine is dry here, so non-issue as formulated; another reason B6 and glutamine both stay dry. (3) Glutamine + heavy citrate load: no precipitation/chelation (glutamine has no divalent-metal chelation role); only an indirect acid-base note. (4) Ammonia released on wet degradation is itself an undesired product (off-flavor, mild toxicity in TPN context) — pair: glutamine -> hydrolysis -> ammonia; mitigation = dry phase only. No conflict with NAC, taurine, betaine, creatine (all dry preload-compatible). Verdict: the ONE real R3 hazard is Maillard with the sugar SKUs — fully avoided by the dry-first design.
avian_evidence: POULTRY EVIDENCE IS GOOD (chronic/dietary, NOT acute-sip). Glutamine is conditionally essential under stress and is a primary fuel for enterocytes and lymphocytes. Dietary supplementation 0.5-1.0% of feed improves intestinal morphology (villus height, barrier integrity), growth, and antioxidant status in broilers, especially under HEAT STRESS. Systematic review + meta-analysis (13 studies): glutamine raised body-weight gain (SMD 0.70), feed intake (SMD 0.64), glutathione (SMD 1.25), GPx (SMD 1.12), SOD (SMD 0.97), catalase (SMD 0.94) and lowered MDA (SMD -0.84) under heat stress [Ncho CM, Gupta V, Choi Y-H. Antioxidants (Basel) 2023;12(3):570, PMID 36978818, DOI 10.3390/antiox12030570, Tier 1]. Heat-stress intestinal-barrier/morphology benefit at 0.5-1.0% feed [Tubitak Vet 2014 broiler study; Poult Sci/J of glutamine-barrier work, Tier 1-2]. MECHANISM: enterocyte fuel + glutathione precursor (gut integrity, antioxidant), relevant to the panting/heat-stressed exerted bird. ACUTE vs CHRONIC: all avian benefit is CHRONIC dietary (days-weeks of gut/immune conditioning) — a between-rounds sip does NOT acutely raise muscle performance; treat as a PRELOAD-phase active, not a fight-day lever. DOSE RANGE: 0.5-1.0% feed ~ 5-10 g/kg feed (~600 mg-1.5 g/bird/day at ~120-150 g intake). MAX TOLERABLE: no avian toxicity/upper-limit reported in the meta-analysis or NRC; as a freely metabolized/ureotelic-cleared amino acid with high enterocyte uptake, moderate excess is not flagged as harmful [PMID 36978818 reports no upper safe limit; NRC poultry gives no Gln tox ceiling — UNKNOWN exact NOAEL]. NOTE: capsule doses here (70-100 mg) are an order of magnitude below the studied chronic-efficacious dietary intake, so the current capsule amount is likely sub-therapeutic for the gut/antioxidant mechanism.
verdict: ADD-conditional, DRY PRELOAD phase only (move/keep out of all liquids). Recommendation: include L-glutamine in the dry feed PRELOAD at ~5-10 g/kg feed (0.5-1.0%, ~0.6-1.5 g/bird/day) for ~7-10 days pre-event for gut-barrier + glutathione/antioxidant conditioning under heat stress — this is where the avian evidence lives. SKIP for the fight-day drink and any wet SKU (Maillard with sugars + hydrolytic deamidation to pyroglutamate+ammonia even within the stable pH window). The current 70-100 mg capsule dose is below chronic-efficacious intake; if kept in capsules, it is acute-window-irrelevant and likely sub-therapeutic — better redeployed as a feed-premix preload dose. Not a fight-day acute lever (no acute ergogenic mechanism in birds). Net: ADD to dry preload at proper dose; do NOT put in the drink; current capsule placement = SKIP/underdosed.
cautions:
- Acute expectation mismatch: handlers may believe a between-rounds glutamine sip boosts performance; avian benefit is chronic gut/immune/antioxidant conditioning, not acute power — set expectations, dose as preload (welfare-neutral, non-blocking note).
- Wet-product ammonia: if glutamine is ever dissolved (especially warm, with sugars), it degrades to pyroglutamate + ammonia — off-flavor, reduced active, and ammonia is undesirable; this is a producibility/quality caution, fully avoided by dry-only handling.
- Hygroscopic-moderate powder: keep desiccated in the dry premix to prevent in-package hydrolysis/clumping.
- Repeated high dietary loading is well tolerated in broiler studies, but exact avian NOAEL/upper limit is UNKNOWN — stay within the studied 0.5-1.0% feed band rather than extrapolating higher.
- Sub-therapeutic risk: at the current 70-100 mg/capsule the gut/antioxidant effect is unlikely to manifest; either dose it properly in feed or drop it to avoid pill burden with no benefit.
sources: Arii K, Kobayashi H, Kai T, Kokuba Y. Degradation kinetics of L-glutamine in aqueous solution. Eur J Pharm Sci. 1999;9(1):75-78. PMID 10493999. DOI 10.1016/s0928-0987(99)00047-0. (Tier 1 — peer-reviewed degradation kinetics: pyroglutamate pathway, pH 5.0-7.5 max stability, Ea ~98.7 kJ/mol, pseudo-first-order)

Ncho CM, Gupta V, Choi Y-H. Effects of Dietary Glutamine Supplementation on Heat-Induced Oxidative Stress in Broiler Chickens: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. Antioxidants (Basel). 2023;12(3):570. PMID 36978818. DOI 10.3390/antiox12030570. (Tier 1 — avian meta-analysis: performance + antioxidant effect sizes, no upper limit reported)

Free glutamine as a major precursor of brown products and fluorophores in Maillard reaction systems. PMID 17006601. (Tier 1 — glutamine amide hydrolysis -> NH3 -> dominant browning precursor with reducing sugars; R3 Maillard basis)

The effects of dietary glutamine supplementation on growth performance and intestinal morphology of broiler chickens reared under hot conditions. Turk J Vet Anim Sci 2014;38(3). journals.tubitak.gov.tr/veterinary/vol38/iss3/7 (Tier 1-2 — 0.5-1.0% feed improves intestinal morphology/growth under heat)

Sigma-Aldrich / ChemicalBook L-Glutamine (CAS 56-85-9) physicochemical datasheet: aqueous solubility ~36 mg/mL (~250 mM) neutral pH ~25C; MW 146.15. (Tier 2 — manufacturer/compendial physicochemical constants)

Parenteral nutrition literature consensus: free L-glutamine omitted from aqueous TPN due to hydrolytic instability (pyroglutamate + ammonia); stabilized dipeptide Ala-Gln used instead. (Tier 2 — review/textbook rationale supporting dry-only handling)
blocking_gaps: Exact half-life of L-glutamine in water at 25C and 4C at formula pH ~6 is UNKNOWN from the primary abstract (full Arii 1999 text behind paywall); the ~0.2%/3d and ~3.3%/45d figures are secondary/model-system (Tier 2/3) and should be confirmed before any wet-formulation decision — but this does not block the recommended DRY placement.
Avian NOAEL / upper tolerable dietary glutamine UNKNOWN (no quantified ceiling in PMID 36978818 or NRC poultry); recommendation stays within studied 0.5-1.0% feed band.

Whether the original capsule intent was gut-support (preload-appropriate) vs an acute lever is UNKNOWN; if acute was intended, glutamine has no avian acute mechanism and should be repositioned or dropped.

_verify.substance: L-Glutamine dossier verified adversarially. Spot-checked all load-bearing citations via WebSearch; all are real and accurately characterized.
corrections: Minor (non-blocking): solubility sources (ChemicalBook/Sigma) report ~35-36 mg/mL at 20C, not 25C as stated; difference is immaterial to the moot/dry-phase R1 verdict.

Minor (cosmetic): the ~0.2%/3d and ~3.3%/45d wet-degradation figures are self-flagged Tier 2/3 secondary and not from the Arii 1999 primary abstract; the dossier already discloses this honestly as a blocking gap for any wet decision.
_verify.notes:
Four load-bearing sources verified as REAL and accurately quoted:

(1) PMID 10493999 / DOI 10.1016/s0928-0987(99)00047-0 (Arii et al, Eur J Pharm Sci 1999) — CONFIRMED. Degradation product = 5-pyrrolidone-2-carboxylic acid (pyroglutamate); pseudo-first-order kinetics; max stability pH 5.0-7.5. Dossier's R2 claims match the abstract exactly, not inverted. Formula target pH 5.8-6.2 correctly placed inside the most-stable window.

(2) PMID 36978818 / DOI 10.3390/antiox12030570 (Ncho, Gupta, Choi, Antioxidants 2023;12(3):570) — CONFIRMED. 13 eligible studies; glutamine improves antioxidant status (GSH, GPx, SOD, catalase up; MDA down) and growth in heat-stressed broilers. Avian framing (conditionally essential, chronic dietary not acute, gut/antioxidant mechanism) is correct and consistent with the avian physiology frame.

(3) PMID 17006601 (Amino Acids, DOI 10.1007/s00726-006-0388-9) — CONFIRMED. Free glutamine is the MOST reactive amine for Maillard browning (420 nm) via dual pathway: amide hydrolysis -> NH3 (major ammonia source vs asparagine/glutamate/urea) PLUS direct alpha-amino Maillard. The dossier's R3 mechanism is exactly right, not fabricated. This is the one genuine R3 hazard and is correctly mitigated by dry-only placement.

(4) Solubility ~36 mg/mL (CAS 56-85-9) — CONFIRMED via ChemicalBook/Sigma (35 mg/mL @20C; 36 g/L). R1 headroom math (36/intended) is arithmetically correct; the N/A-because-dry framing is the right call since glutamine is a dry capsule ingredient.

No inversions, no fabricated PMIDs, no hallucinated numbers. The verdict (DRY PRELOAD only, never the drink, current 70-100 mg capsule dose sub-therapeutic vs 0.5-1.0% feed) is internally consistent and avian-grounded. Blocking gaps (paywalled half-life, unknown avian NOAEL, unclear original capsule intent) are honestly disclosed and do not undermine the dry placement. Cautions are written as welfare/quality notes, non-blocking, appropriate. Dossier accepted as-is.
